speaker: interviewer
text: March 7, 1995. The survivor is Adam Schwartz. Tape number two. You were talking about a gentleman who basically, you said, saved your life in the ghetto. What time of the year was this? When was this? Do you remember the date or the time of year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the moment I come in the ghetto, I lost all mind. I say, it's just nonsense to live tomorrow. I'd be better off today. And the-- you know, the-- what I lived with them, you know, they was in the same shul like me. And they say, look, maybe still tomorrow be the sun shine. Anyway, was-- [INAUDIBLE] I rounded enough clothes, make clothes. And I was thinking the men will say, you'll be safe there because you work for the government, what they wanted. I work maybe three weeks, the same thing. They're going-- they're going to liquidate everything. They're going to liquidate the ghetto. They put us on the cows, the wagons. And they sent me to in another pensionnat, you know, to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: I come down-- I didn't-- can't go down when they put us in. I see ladies, their bellies and their-- all of-- they all make the shit, I don't know how to say on the floor. And I say next, I say, let me go in here. I didn't-- can't move with some lady threw up and some lady did like this. We stay maybe a day until they open the doors because they to have to clean up the transport before that we were staying in the wagons. Meantime, so many of us suffocate--

speaker: interviewer
text: Suffocating.

speaker: interviewee
text: Suffocate. OK, I said, we wait and we be shot. One time is coming, I see, start to be morning like 8 o'clock, come some young mens with big muscles. And I was thinking that this is Jews. You know, and there was Jews. They would call them kapo, kapos. And [INAUDIBLE] and then was not a Gestapo man. [NON-ENGLISH] who was there, and he had a little stick, you see. With the little stick he decide your life. Right, left, right, left. And this was so schnell, so quick, you didn't-- can't do-- so fast, he did that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you know what Auschwitz was before you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. When I opened the door, when the train go in, I saw-- and I was thinking these manikins on the-- on the electric wires. [INAUDIBLE] about and we looked at. And the other guy, no, this isn't people is there laying. Like fish. I say, you know, you have to go, you have to go. It's too late to cry. So look how lucky I was. OK, I sleep-- was one Hungarian, he beat us. We didn't sleep right on the floor, he beat us with a stick like this. He say, we don't die today. We die tomorrow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one time, a soldier, a general, he took out maybe 12 people. Where is [NON-ENGLISH] And I tell-- [NON-ENGLISH] you are tailor. Where is a carpenter? You know, a carpenter. [INAUDIBLE] 12 people. Out! I was only three days in Auschwitz. Three days. And where did--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the luckiest guy in the whole world.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, now is-- they sent out-- Let me calm because I get a little excited.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- they needed you to work somewhere at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- yeah, they need you. You know, like you take me out, and they need me. Anyway, I make-- I skipped-- this is coming back to me, the [INAUDIBLE] what I was there. And a guy, the commander, a German, he was not a Gestapo, a regular soldier. He took me and one by the name Bochan and one Saliniki-- Saliniki-- Saliniki. He didn't speak Jewish. He speak only Hebrew. Greek. Saliniki. He didn't understand nothing. He looked at me and I looked at him. And he looked at the-- he was a Polish [INAUDIBLE]. I looked at-- with him I didn't [INAUDIBLE]. The only thing was I can talk to him and I say, who you are? And he say, [NON-ENGLISH SINGING]

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: We sleep together. Then come another officer. From the 12, we was only three. Bochan and Saliniki. One supposed to be a carpenter and the other one supposed to be a cook, good. And I was the tailor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they take you when you left Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to remember-- It's coming back to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot the name. I forgot the name. Anyway, from this time-- I don't can't say, I am bad because I deliver-- in the kitchen was cans to handle and food for the guy, [INAUDIBLE] of us. There was maybe a half a mile. We put in a steak. And this was what you-- maybe you saw what the milk the cows, as a can. You know, two cans. [NON-ENGLISH] And we took this to the guy was he [INAUDIBLE] of us. And meantime you see-- and meantime, we supposed to take him back the cans. We took the hat from my-- what we had was our hats, you know. And we dumped over the can and we grimmel out. This was our food. [INAUDIBLE] talking to you now till the liberation come up.

speaker: interviewer
text: The liberation come up and I saw the Germans start to disappear, little by little. And everybody say-- one from Krakow, a guy-- everybody by themselves, have to save for yourself. One go here and-- where I have to go? You put me down [INAUDIBLE]. Was I to know to go right or the left? Bochan-- I say I want to looking for him. We go-- we didn't know. And we see Germans walking over there. We lay down. Here we hungry. What we can doing? Say, let's take a chance.

speaker: interviewee
text: Meantime, we come in the house, in a German house. She say, come in my house-- don't do nothing to me, please don't beat me. I was-- I didn't can't walk on my feet. And she said--

speaker: interviewer
text: And she thought you were going to beat her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't beat her up. And he say to me, you know, I think the Germans disappear. And I think, come in another. And I say, I don't can't make no because I didn't [INAUDIBLE]. I was with maybe 80 pounds. The food was left was the German, the first one give me. He [INAUDIBLE] as I have to-- the craft to survive. We come-- come of a place, of another place. The German put in load of food. Another guy start to eat, he die. He get right away. He's not supposed to eat. And he did so eat that we took potatoes and we eat the potatoes. Later was [NON-ENGLISH], one go to Germany was already a blue cross. And we didn't know it. And right away the Pollocks start to take who want to go, let's go with the wagon, let's go. And I took the wagon and I go to Lodz. I come to Lodz. It's 10-- 10 miles to Teczyn, you know. Let me go back. I come back. I was thinking you dead already, the Pollock, you know-- I'm still-- oh, stay here. And I looked through the window and I see what the father put in the bushes, all the bushes. And I say, I don't stay, I have to go back. He delivered me back to Lodz. After this time, I never saw him. And meantime, you know, the HIAS used to keep care of us. We can talk. I told you the food was I got in me was when the first time was the Germans [INAUDIBLE] so fast. And later I meet--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you think that was what--

speaker: interviewee
text: I meet-- Helen was the same thing. She come from another-- from another [NON-ENGLISH]. And she have her bundle. She looked at me, I looked at her. What is next?

speaker: interviewer
text: They give us room, this is true, you know, in Lodz. What you do with the rooms? You could look at the rooms just like this and they tell us to come back 12 o'clock. We eat, we can eat. In this way I meet Helen. I have a hard time to meet her. She have [NON-ENGLISH] I have lot of competitors, let's put this way. A lot of competition?

speaker: interviewee
text: Competition. I try so hard. She didn't know. She had a brother to-- you know, everybody [INAUDIBLE]. And she looked at [INAUDIBLE] paper, maybe I can find the two brothers. And one time, maybe two month, three months later, is coming a list from Italy, he survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your older brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: About that, hold that thought. And it's going by. I didn't think my brother survive. I was thinking he dead because he was working in [? Posen ?] [INAUDIBLE], the hard labor trucks. One time in the HIAS, they're saying it's two brothers, [NON-ENGLISH]. They look for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were looking for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were looking for me. That means somebody's left. A sister, a brother, a niece. He didn't want to come anymore back. In Italy right away come to New York. We have a cousin, you know, [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, maybe far cousin. He took the-- he helped them a little bit, because he give him right away a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: But your brothers did not want to go back to Poland? They-- they didn't want to go see what their hometown was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I give you, since you are American-- did you have animal in your life?

speaker: interviewer
text: An animal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I never did.

speaker: interviewee
text: Beat up a animal, call him back. This is the answer darling, which you'll give me. Beat him up. You see if he want to go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your brothers never wanted to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. Yeah, darling-- [INAUDIBLE] because I mean I [INAUDIBLE] the little things. What I go in between, there's no end. I can stay three months with you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: What you missed is the name for my laager. I give-- it's coming back to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it's that's coming back to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but now I'm swimming.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because there's a lot of information.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing else hits me so much. When the two kids tried to say, Uncle, Uncle--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your two nephews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uncle, what they doing to me? Look at. Even my wife, I didn't tell her all this as I tell you. I didn't tell everything because I make her sick and I don't want to talk about. She have her baggage and I have my baggage. Darling, I come to so low was you say you shoot me. Go ahead. My pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you never--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH], the laager. We stay in that train-- closed, the train. We stay maybe for three days, closed, inside, suffocate.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was when you were going to Auschwitz? That's when you were being transported?

speaker: interviewee
text: This time, darling, the life is going so low you didn't care how your life. You kill me? Puh. You make a joke.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, do you think because you had a trade, because you were a tailor--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That it really saved your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the energy was I have is the German was, I told you, he took us home. We were so fat, they like to say-- fat with our belly like [? don't ?] [? mind ?] [? this. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: But because of that, it helped sustain you later on--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the way, it was like this. I looked at them in Auschwitz-- and I was after six, seven days, by the way, it comes out, swollen in the feet. And here I get on the bumps, here by the eyes. Three weeks ago I have operation, you know. I took off the cataracts. What I-- now I feel better. I give out a little.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel better.

speaker: interviewer
text: Since the war, what has your life been like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, here?

speaker: interviewer
text: Here or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I like it. I come into this family, like her. Her son had the biggest-- the biggest in Toronto. Then you say he changed the name. Not Boreshofsky, Doctor Boren. Nobody can come to him. You have to wait six months for-- for-- and my son is doing good too, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how--

speaker: interviewee
text: He is no [INAUDIBLE], my youngest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so how many children do have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three.

speaker: interviewer
text: Three.

speaker: interviewee
text: One daughter and two boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five.

speaker: interviewer
text: Five.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's put all the pictures in boxes, out of the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. What message do you have for your grandchildren? What would you like them to know about what you went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm glad that you ask me this question. I have a doctor, he say don't mention to the kids nothing. When it's come on a holiday, a Jewish holiday, and the kids ask me-- oh, I'm so mad of this doctor. Everybody have a grandpa. Why I don't have a grandpa? Do I say, where's your ball? Let's go play ball. No, I tell you about me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was days when we legal marijuana, I be the first one to buy. You get the point what I mean. Later, to come out now, tell the kids everything. And all the time the kids ask me-- the youngest, my son, the doctor say, Why you all the time play boloney when I talk my Grandpa? All the time you jump in on another route. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: The doctor told me. I did like the doctor told me. He's wrong.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The kids have to know. The kids have to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You've never told your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you're telling them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never told them. It was the biggest mistake. I go what the doctor told me. And another thing, what I'm good with my life what I did. I was so busy. I come in the neighborhood, I open a shop, and I did, darling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were a tailor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many many. Even the [NON-ENGLISH], they're jealous of me, darling. [NON-ENGLISH]. She have two kids-- one the grocers from Toronto. The biggest furrier what they used to be. Now that they're now, millionaires. Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, what is it you want your grandchildren--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now is the only thing I should wake up every morning. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's for sure. What is it that you want your grandchildren to know about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: That now they find it out anyway. They find out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well--

speaker: interviewee
text: And some they say, take it easy. I'm still doing work because, I tell you the truth, I feel better when I work when I don't do nothing. Because at night time, this night she say, [NON-ENGLISH], Why you scream so high? I see the Gestapo one time grab me by the hair, you know. I didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you never forget that? You-- do you think about this often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now you're talking. I don't know how to answer to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think about it all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember how did my sisters get. One sister was raped. What you talking about? A beautiful sister. Five beautiful sisters, model sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were they all killed? Were they killed in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Around [INAUDIBLE] children. You see, when you didn't have children, you have a chance to survive. Now, when you have children-- there was some mommies, some son of a guns take you away. They come in, they lay-- they give away the children to you, and she go the other way. But not every mama can do this. If my child go, I go. Yeah. Look, the bottom line is this. Germany is still Germany. And the damage was done. You don't can't blow it away. I hope I didn't disturb you with my talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, you didn't. No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because in my store I never want a politic. When it's come in some day Germans, some they must think I'm German because I speak very good German. I told you one time Yom Kippur I say I closed. I lost a friend. He is in the oil business, [NON-ENGLISH], a Gentile. I lost it. That's [INAUDIBLE]. Now the kids take it easy. Daddy, don't do nothing. Give him a ride. Don't even do-- sometimes she say, he's not to know. And she thinks she doing me a favor.

speaker: interviewer
text: She don't do me a favor. I feel better when I do something. The mind is busy. Yeah. I don't want to remember, I told you. Where is marijuana now? I buy it. I be the first one to buy. But yet you want your grandchildren to know what you went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never want to hurt them, darling. I don't know what I can do. But I get pimples when I start to wake up this conversation. Let's put this-- I like about [NON-ENGLISH], you knows, weddings, this, this. To be-- how you used to say, doped. To being doped all the time, doped. I'm not ashamed. I be the first one to buy marijuana.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Is there anything else that you want to add? Anything else you want to have said on the tape during this interview?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I hope I can wake up every morning. Say hallelujah, that's all. That's what I hope to be. Now is health, darling. And health is hard to buy.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can't buy that. You can't buy that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hard to buy health. When you're young, you try, you know. Come in this age. My brother was so strong, like a horse. Now he's cracking down too.

speaker: interviewer
text: But some of your health problems are as a result of the Holocaust and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now-- oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: The conditions that [INTERPOSING VOICES] you lived through?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to take medicine. Taking medicine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Schwartz, thank you very much for doing this.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a pleasure, both of you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Darling. You have life. Have to continue, they say. Was yesterday--
